title: Epidemic Preparedness and Response
text:
Preface

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: Epidemic threshold
text:
In applied surveillance, epidemic threshold is a statistical or operational cut-off separating “expected/endemic” from “excess/epidemic” counts.
there are two types of thresholds used to initiate response: an alert threshold and an epidemic threshold (WHO 2019)

Alert threshold: a lower cut-off whose, whose crossing triggers heightened vigilance, investigation and preparedness (but not yet full response). such as when there is one suspected case (as for an epidemic-prone disease or for a disease targeted for elimination or eradication) or when there is an unexplained increase for any disease or unusual pattern seen over a period of time in weekly or monthly summary reporting.
epidemic threshold: a higher cut-off whose crossing confirms epidemic activity and triggers control/response measures. For rare diseases or diseases targeted for eradication, detection of a single case suggests an epidemic. In such situations, one case is unusual and is a serious event. In other situations, a number of cases will trigger a response.

FOr example, alert threshold is 2nd quartile/Median of confirmed malaria cases for the past five years, epidemic threshold for malaria in some countries is 3rd Quartile of confirmed malaria cases (WHO 2019).
These threshold measure how deviation the current situation compared to a Endemic / baseline level: the expected, normal background frequency of a disease in a place/season (Disease Control and Prevention 2012).
This was first mentioned by Serfling “at a distance of 1.64 standard deviations above the trend line, a level which experience has shown to be useful for distinguishing epidemic increase from random variation.” (Serfling 1963). Observations above it indicate epidemic/excess activity. The Serfling approach remains the basis of CDC’s influenza epidemic threshold and excess-mortality estimation.




Disease Control, Centers for, and Prevention. 2012. “Principles of Epidemiology in Public Health Practice.” In.


Serfling, Robert E. 1963. “Methods for Current Statistical Analysis of Excess Pneumonia-Influenza Deaths.” Public Health Reports 78 (6): 494–506. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1915276/.


WHO. 2019. “Technical Guidelines for Integrated Disease Surveillance and Response in the African Region: Third Edition.” https://www.afro.who.int/publications/technical-guidelines-integrated-disease-surveillance-and-response-african-region-third.

title: 1  CUSUM
text:
1.1 Computing an epidemic threshold with CUSUM
CUSUM accumulates deviations from an expected mean and is therefore optimal for detecting small, persistent shifts (the gradual onset of an outbreak)
CUSUM was invented by ES. Page in 1954 (Page 1954) for industrial statistical process control; it entered epidemiology via Tillett & Spencer’s 1982 influenza-surveillance “cusum graphs” in England and Wales (Tillett and Spencer 1982)
Page referred to a “quality number” \(\theta\), by which he meant a parameter of the probability distribution; for example, the mean. He devised CUSUM as a method to determine changes in it, and proposed a criterion for deciding when to take corrective action.
For case counts the standard tool is the CUSUM in the R surveillance package (algo.cusum) (Rossi, Lampugnani, and Marchi 1999), building on counted-data CUSUMs (Lucas 1985). It works in three steps, shown live below, using the package’s default settings.
1. Expected count. First estimate the in-control mean \(m\), the count we expect when nothing unusual is happening. By default (m = NULL) the package uses the mean of the weeks before monitoring begins; for seasonal data it can instead fit a Poisson regression \(\log m_t = \alpha + \gamma\cos(2\pi t/p) + \delta\sin(2\pi t/p)\) and predict each week (m = "glm").
2. Standardize. Each new count is turned into a standardized deviation (the default trans = "standard"),
\[ z_t = \frac{x_t - m}{\sqrt{m}}, \]
so a healthy week sits near \(z_t \approx 0\) whatever the size of \(m\).
3. Accumulate. The chart adds up these deviations minus a reference value \(k\), floored at zero, and signals once it reaches the decision boundary \(h\):
\[ S_t = \max\!\left(0,\; S_{t-1} + (z_t - k)\right), \qquad \text{signal when } S_t \ge h. \]
The package defaults are \(k = 1.04\) and \(h = 2.26\) (tuned for an in-control run length of roughly 500 weeks), and by default \(S_t\) is not reset after a signal, so a whole epidemic period stays flagged.
The same rule can be read as a threshold on the raw count: a week signals exactly when
\[ x_t \;\ge\; U_t = \left\lceil m + \sqrt{m}\,(h + k - S_{t-1}) \right\rceil, \]
which the package returns as its upperbound. This \(U_t\) is the epidemic threshold in cases for that week. It starts high and falls as evidence accumulates, so a sustained rise is caught even when no single week looks extreme. Drag across the chart below to move through the weeks; the CUSUM follows.

title: 1  CUSUM
text:
Lucas, James M. 1985. “Counted Data CUSUM’s.” Technometrics 27 (2): 129–44. https://doi.org/10.1080/00401706.1985.10488030.


Page, E. S. 1954. “Continuous Inspection Schemes.” Biometrika 41 (1/2): 100–115. https://doi.org/10.2307/2333009.


Rossi, G., L. Lampugnani, and M. Marchi. 1999. “An Approximate CUSUM Procedure for Surveillance of Health Events.” Statistics in Medicine 18 (16): 2111–22. https://doi.org/10.1002/(SICI)1097-0258(19990830)18:16<2111::AID-SIM171>3.0.CO;2-Q.


Tillett, H. E., and I. L. Spencer. 1982. “Influenza Surveillance in England and Wales Using Routine Statistics. Development of ’Cusum’ Graphs to Compare 12 Previous Winters and to Monitor the 1980/81 Winter.” The Journal of Hygiene 88 (1): 83–94. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0022172400069928.
